text: The Good Samaritan in the Gospel of Luke is one of the most recognizable parables in the Christian tradition. While existing scholarship frequently analyzes the narrative through the lens of ethnic tension, this study seeks to re-evaluate the text by prioritizing the impact of religious discord. The historical animosity between Jews and Samaritans was deeply rooted in theological disputes, exemplified by the forced imposition of Judaism upon the Samaritans during the reign of John Hyrcanus. Utilizing a descriptive analytical approach, this research argues that the parable transcends sectarian boundaries. The findings suggest that the narrative serves as a profound call for universal human values, emphasizing social equality, radical compassion, and a totality of service that overrides historical and religious divides.
